title: About
text:
Hi! I am Cassie Nesheim, a junior at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln majoring in Data Science with a minor in Sociology. I am passionate about using data to uncover insights, tell meaningful stories, and support better decision-making, especially in the world of sports.
I have experience working with Python and R to clean, analyze, and visualize data, along with a strong foundation in statistics. My analysis work goes beyond the basics, incorporating methods like hypothesis testing and other statistical techniques to draw deeper, more reliable, confident conclusions.
As a former gymnast, I bring a unique perspective to sports analytics, particularly in gymnastics. This blog features quite a bit of gymnastics analysis, along with a variety of other sports. View my posts to see some of my work!

title: Does location actually matter for gymnastics scoring in the SEC? Is home judging bias real?
text:
Gymnastics fans have accused judges to have “home bias” for decades. According to the gymternet (the mass of internet gymnastics enthusiasts), when teams are at home, the judges are noticeably more lax with their judging and often award “gifts,” or scores that are higher than they should have been. For example, if a gymnast takes a medium step on their landing but otherwise has no other errors that is a -0.1 deduction. That routine will typically score around a 9.85 or a 9.9, but at home that routine might score a 9.95 which is an impossible score.
Well, let’s dig in to this issue and see if home judging bias is real and if it is, who benefits from it the most. To investigate this issue, we are going to dive into the SEC, noteably one of the more dominant conferences in NCAA women’s gymnastics. There are 9 SEC women’s gymnastics teams, 7 of which are currently ranked in the top 10.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(ggbeeswarm)
library(ggbump)

sec_reg <- read_csv("data/sec_reg_season.csv")
rankings <- read_csv("data/rtn_rankings.csv")

sec_away <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Location == "A") |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(VAULT, BARS, BEAM, FLOOR),
    names_to = "Event",
    values_to = "Score"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(Score), Gymnast=="Team Totals", Score>=49) |>
  mutate(Event = factor(Event, levels=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR")))
  
sec_home <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Location == "H") |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(VAULT, BARS, BEAM, FLOOR),
    names_to = "Event",
    values_to = "Score"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(Score), Gymnast=="Team Totals", Score>=49) |>
  mutate(Event = factor(Event, levels=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR")))

sec <- c("Georgia", "LSU", "Florida", "Kentucky", "Missouri", "Alabama", "Arkansas", "Auburn", "Oklahoma")

sec_ranks <- rankings |>
  filter(
    Team %in% sec
  )

ranking_10 <- rankings |>
  filter(Rank <= 10)

sec10_ranks <- rankings |>
  filter(
    Team %in% sec,
    Rank <= 10
  )

ratings_bump <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bump(data=rankings, aes(x=Week, y=Rank, group=Team), color="lightgrey") + 
  geom_bump(data=sec_ranks, aes(x=Week, y=Rank, group=Team), color="blue") +
  geom_point(data=rankings, aes(x=Week, y=Rank), color="lightgrey", size = 2) + 
  geom_point(data=sec_ranks, aes(x=Week, y=Rank), color="blue", size = 2) +
  geom_text(data = rankings |> filter(Week == min(Week)), aes(x = Week - .2, y=Rank, label = Team), size = 3, hjust = 1) +
  geom_text(data = rankings |> filter(Week == max(Week)), aes(x = Week + .2, y=Rank, label = Team), size = 3, hjust = 0) +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  labs(title="SEC dominates college gymnastics.", 
       subtitle="The top three teams for NCAA gymnastics have consistently been from the SEC throughout the 2026 Regular Season.", 
       y= "Rank", 
       x = "Week",
    caption="Source: RoadToNationals | By Cassie Nesheim"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = ,
    plot.subtitle = ,
    axis.title.x = ,
    axis.title.y = ,
    panel.grid.major = element_blank()
    ) 

ratings_bump









It is clear that the SEC produces some dominant gymnastics teams, making them the perfect initial case study to determine whether home judging bias is real.
Now, let’s dig into what we actually care about. Do gymnasts score better when they are judged at home than when they are judged away?


Code
#i want to add a circle around the home floor
away_dist <- ggplot(data = sec_away, aes(x = Event, y = Score)) +
  geom_beeswarm(color = "lightgray") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "errorbar", aes(ymax = after_stat(y), ymin = after_stat(y)),
    width = .75, color = "red", linewidth = 1
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "text", aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", after_stat(y))),
    vjust = -0.75, color="black", size = 2
  ) +
  labs(subtitle="Away") +
  theme_minimal()

home_dist <- ggplot(data = sec_home, aes(x = Event, y = Score, group = Event)) +
  geom_beeswarm(color = "lightgray") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "errorbar", aes(ymax = after_stat(y), ymin = after_stat(y)),
    width = .75, color = "red", linewidth = 1
  )  +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "text", aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", after_stat(y))),
    vjust = -0.75, color="black", size = 2
  ) +
  labs(subtitle="Home") +
  theme_minimal()

away_dist + home_dist +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Floor shows biggest difference in home vs away scoring",
    subtitle = "While the averages show scores for gymnasts to be mostly similar when scored at home vs away, \nfloor scores are noticeably higher when teams are at home.",
    caption = "Source: Virtius and RoadToNationals | By Cassie Nesheim"
  )  & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









In college gymnastics, most of the scores range from 9.7-10.0, 10.0 being a perfect score. Because of this tight scoring range, it is slightly difficult to see the differences between home and away scoring at first glance. However, the red bar (each event’s average) is noticeably higher for floor at home than it is when away. The other events don’t show too big of differences, so let’s hone in on floor.
Well, it sort of makes sense for floor to be scored higher at home than away. When teams are at home, they almost always compete floor last. In college gymnastics, it is also believed that as the competition goes on scores get looser. Meaning, higher scores are awarded easier for potentially worse routines than at the beginning of competition. So, as floor is last at home this makes sense.
Also, if home bias is a real thing, judges may be trying to overcompensate in the last event to ensure the home team wins. I am not saying this is what judges are doing, but it does make sense for a potential reason that only floor seems to have a noticeable difference when scored away vs at home.
So, essentially we have found that between the gymnasts individual scores, there is not a huge difference in home vs away scoring for every event except floor. But, are these results actually significant? Can we make actual conclusions about this data? I ran a significance test to determine if Location actually effects individual gymansts scores.


Code
library(gt)

t_sec_team <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01)

fl_team <- t_sec_team |>
  filter(Team=="Florida")

fl_team_test <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=fl_team)$p.value

lsu_team <- t_sec_team |>
  filter(Team=="LSU")

lsu_team_test <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=lsu_team)$p.value

ok_team <- t_sec_team |>
  filter(Team=="Oklahoma")

ok_team_test <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=ok_team)$p.value

aub_team <- t_sec_team |>
  filter(Team=="Auburn")

aub_team_test <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=aub_team)$p.value

geo_team <- t_sec_team |>
  filter(Team=="Georgia")

geo_team_test <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=geo_team)$p.value

ark_team <- t_sec_team |>
  filter(Team=="Arkansas")

ark_team_test <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=ark_team)$p.value

miss_team <- t_sec_team |>
  filter(Team=="Missouri")

miss_team_test <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=miss_team)$p.value

kent_team <- t_sec_team |>
  filter(Team=="Kentucky")

kent_team_test <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=kent_team)$p.value

bama_team <- t_sec_team |>
  filter(Team=="Alabama")

bama_team_test <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=bama_team)$p.value

teams <- c("Florida", "LSU", "Oklahoma", "Auburn", "Georgia", "Arkansas", "Missouri", "Kentucky", "Alabama")
team_p_values <- c(fl_team_test, lsu_team_test, ok_team_test, aub_team_test, geo_team_test, ark_team_test, miss_team_test, kent_team_test, bama_team_test)

team_pvalue <- data.frame(teams, team_p_values) |>
  arrange(team_p_values)

team_pvalue |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    team_p_values = "P-Values"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Does Home Judging Bias Actually Have Signficance?",
    subtitle = "Oklahoma is the only team in the SEC where home judging actually has a significant effect \non how well their team scores."
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**By:** Cassie Nesheim  |  **Source:** Virtius and RoadtoNationals")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "indianred1"),
      cell_text(color = "black")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = team_p_values<0.05)
  )








Does Home Judging Bias Actually Have Signficance?


Oklahoma is the only team in the SEC where home judging actually has a significant effect on how well their team scores.


teams

A significance test reveals that there is a 6.251% this difference in average score at away meets than at home meets is due to random chance, which is not enough significance for us to say there is an actual difference and location has an affect on scoring for teams in the SEC.
However,


Code
#not helpful?? likely??
sec_home_9.5 <- sec_home |>
  filter(Score >= 9.8)

sec_away_9.5 <- sec_away |>
  filter(Score >= 9.8)

away_dist_9.5 <- ggplot(data = sec_away_9.5, aes(x = Event, y = Score)) +
  geom_beeswarm(color = "lightgray") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "errorbar", aes(ymax = after_stat(y), ymin = after_stat(y)),
    width = .75, color = "red", linewidth = 1
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "text", aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", after_stat(y))),
    vjust = -0.75, color="black", size = 2
  ) +
  labs(subtitle="Away") +
  theme_minimal()

home_dist_9.5 <- ggplot(data = sec_home_9.5, aes(x = Event, y = Score, group = Event)) +
  geom_beeswarm(color = "lightgray") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "errorbar", aes(ymax = after_stat(y), ymin = after_stat(y)),
    width = .75, color = "red", linewidth = 1
  )  +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "text", aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", after_stat(y))),
    vjust = -0.75, color="black", size = 2
  ) +
  labs(subtitle="Home") +
  theme_minimal()

away_dist_9.5 + home_dist_9.5









teams compete 6 routines in each event and they count the highest scoring 5 routines, essentially dropping the lowest scoring routine
